message:
fix(disk_pressure): fail closed on NaN samples in Ewma, PID, and tier classifier (ft-761tz)

Three separate NaN-propagation paths in the disk-pressure monitor turned
a single unreadable /data sample into a permanently-poisoned monitor.

## The underlying footgun: f64::clamp(NaN, _, _) returns NaN

The docs on std::f64::clamp warn that NaN propagates, but every clamp
call in this file was written as if clamp would sanitize the input. It
doesn't. Every `>=` comparison against NaN is also false, so any branch
that classifies pressure via `usage_fraction >= threshold` falls through
silently to the "safest" branch — which in our classifier's case was
GREEN (healthy), i.e. exactly the wrong answer for an unreadable disk.

## Fix 1: EwmaEstimator::update — skip NaN samples

crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs (EwmaEstimator)

Before: `let sample = sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0);` kept NaN, then
`self.value = alpha * sample + (1.0 - alpha) * self.value` set self.value
to NaN. Every subsequent update preserves the NaN because
`alpha * NaN + (1 - alpha) * prev` is still NaN.

After: early-return `self.value` on NaN input (no state change). The
caller gets a best-effort read of the prior clean sample rather than an
unrecoverable poison. The first-sample NaN case stays at the default 0.0
because `initialized` stays false.

## Fix 2: PidController::update — return zero on NaN

crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs (PidController)

Before: NaN error taints both integral and derivative. Even if the
caller is careful about clamping the control signal at the output, the
internal state is already gone.

After: if `error.is_nan()` or `dt_secs.is_nan()`, emit 0.0 (no-op
control signal) and leave internal state untouched. The next clean
sample continues from the last good integral/derivative — the monitor
self-heals without operator intervention.

## Fix 3: classify_tier — fail closed to BLACK on NaN

crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs (classify_tier)

Before: NaN usage_fraction survives the clamp, every
`>=` comparison is false, so the classifier fell through all the amber
tiers and returned GREEN. That's the exact OPPOSITE of fail-closed — we
were silently reporting a healthy disk for an unreadable one.

After: short-circuit `is_nan()` -> `DiskPressureTier::Black` BEFORE the
clamp. Rationale (inline comment): when we cannot prove the disk is
safe the only sound answer is to alert operators and let the next clean
sample reclassify. BLACK specifically, not RED, because BLACK is the
"I have no signal" tier — RED implies a measured-bad reading.

## Regression tests

Seven new tests in the existing `tests` module pin the fail-closed
contract so a future refactor that reintroduces any of the three
propagation paths is caught immediately:

  - ft_761tz_ewma_rejects_nan_sample_and_preserves_prior_value:
    feed 0.42, then NaN, then 0.50; assert the NaN is dropped and the
    subsequent 0.50 blends against 0.42 (EWMA arithmetic check, 1e-9
    tolerance).

  - ft_761tz_ewma_rejects_nan_first_sample: first sample NaN keeps the
    estimator uninitialised (value=0.0), next clean sample initialises
    cleanly without a NaN blend.

  - plus 5 more pinning PidController's zero-on-NaN behavior across
    (NaN error, finite dt), (finite error, NaN dt), (NaN, NaN),
    recovery from NaN, and state-preservation during the NaN branch;
    plus classify_tier's NaN -> BLACK and BLACK-is-not-Green pins.

All new tests are deterministic and hermetic. No I/O, no clocks, no
filesystem access — they exercise the arithmetic surface directly.

## Test module wiring

Add `DiskSample` and `std::time::{Duration, Instant}` to the
`use super::{...}` import list so the new controller/monitor tests can
construct samples and drive Instant deltas without reaching out to the
prod clock.

Tracks ft-761tz (NaN-sample fail-closed in disk_pressure monitor).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
